question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted environment variable with a 'hedera'-prefixed name (e.g., HEDERA_MIRROR_GRPC_HOST) to cause it to be silently migrated into the 'hiero' namespace via migrate(), overriding a legitimate hiero property that was already set, leading to unauthorized configuration override?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a property named 'HEDERA_HEDERA_SOMETHING' to exploit the single-replacement logic in convertEnvironmentVariable() (replace count=1), causing the migrated name to become 'HIERO_HEDERA_SOMETHING' instead of the expected 'HIERO_HIERO_SOMETHING', resulting in a misconfigured property binding that bypasses intended security controls?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: convertPropertyName()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a property name like 'hedera.hedera.datasource.password' to exploit the single-occurrence replacement (Strings.CI.replace(..., 1)) in convertPropertyName(), producing 'hiero.hedera.datasource.password' instead of 'hiero.hiero.datasource.password', causing the migrated property to bind to an unintended configuration key?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the case-insensitive prefix check (Strings.CI.startsWith(name, 'hedera')) to inject a property named 'HEDERA_MIRROR_DB_PASSWORD' that gets migrated to 'HIERO_MIRROR_DB_PASSWORD', overwriting a previously configured hiero database password and causing the application to connect to an attacker-controlled database?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence any enumerable property source (e.g., via a config file on a shared filesystem) inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property that gets migrated and inserted via addAfter(), effectively injecting a new property source into the Spring environment before application startup completes, leading to privilege escalation through configuration manipulation?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a property value containing Spring EL expressions (e.g., '#{T(java.lang.Runtime).getRuntime().exec('id')}') as the value of a 'hedera'-prefixed property, which is then copied verbatim via propertySource.getProperty(name) into the migrated MapPropertySource without sanitization, leading to expression injection if the value is later evaluated by a Spring component?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property with a null or empty value to cause the migrated MapPropertySource to contain a null-valued entry, which when bound to a non-nullable Spring configuration property causes a NullPointerException or application startup failure, resulting in a denial-of-service?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a very large number of 'hedera'-prefixed properties (e.g., thousands of environment variables) to cause the LinkedHashMap in migrate() to grow unboundedly, consuming excessive heap memory during ApplicationEnvironmentPreparedEvent processing and causing an OutOfMemoryError that denies service to all users?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a property name with an extremely long string prefixed with 'hedera' to cause Strings.CI.replace() to perform expensive string operations, leading to CPU exhaustion during startup and a denial-of-service condition?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property whose migrated 'hiero' key collides with an existing hiero property in the migrated MapPropertySource, causing the LinkedHashMap to silently overwrite the earlier entry and resulting in an unpredictable final configuration state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getOrder() returns Ordered.LOWEST_PRECEDENCE to ensure that other ApplicationListeners process the environment before migration occurs, and then inject a 'hedera' property that overrides a 'hiero' property that was already consumed by an earlier listener, causing a split-brain configuration state?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a property named exactly 'hedera' (no suffix) to pass the Strings.CI.startsWith(name, 'hedera') check, and then have convertPropertyName() replace 'hedera.' with 'hiero.' on a name that has no dot, producing an unexpected migrated key that binds to an unintended configuration property?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: convertEnvironmentVariable()] Can an unprivileged external user inject an environment variable named 'hedera_mirror_something' (all lowercase) and exploit the case-insensitive replace (Strings.CI.replace) to produce 'hiero_mirror_something', which when wrapped in a SystemEnvironmentPropertySource is mapped by SystemEnvironmentPropertyMapper to an unexpected configuration key, bypassing intended property binding restrictions?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property source name that, after appending DEFAULT_PROPERTY_SOURCE_NAME_SUFFIX ('-hiero'), collides with an existing property source name in the MutablePropertySources, causing addAfter() to throw an exception or silently replace the existing source, leading to denial-of-service or configuration corruption?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user who can influence the property source iteration order exploit the fact that propertySources is modified (via addAfter) during iteration in onApplicationEvent(), potentially causing a ConcurrentModificationException or infinite loop that crashes the application at startup?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property with a value that is a reference to another property (e.g., '${some.secret}') which Spring resolves lazily, causing the migrated property to resolve to a sensitive value from a different property source that the attacker could not directly read, effectively leaking secrets through the migration mechanism?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property that maps to a security-critical hiero configuration key (e.g., hiero.mirror.importer.network) via convertPropertyName(), causing the application to switch to an attacker-controlled network endpoint without any validation of the migrated value?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of value validation in migrate() to inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property with a malformed URL or path as its value, which is then migrated to a 'hiero' key and used by the application to connect to an attacker-controlled server, enabling SSRF?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the migrated property source is inserted with addAfter() (lower priority than the original) to understand that a 'hedera' property in a high-priority source will produce a migrated 'hiero' property at a lower priority, and then inject a separate low-priority 'hiero' property that gets overridden by the migration, causing confusion about which value is actually used?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property whose name contains special characters (e.g., newlines, null bytes, Unicode) that are not filtered by Strings.CI.startsWith(), causing the migrated property name in the MapPropertySource to be malformed and potentially exploitable by downstream Spring property resolution logic?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property with a value containing CRLF sequences to exploit log injection via the log.warn() call that logs properties.keySet(), causing false log entries that mislead security monitoring and hide attacker activity?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a property named 'hedera' (matching startsWith check) with a value that, after migration via convertPropertyName(), produces a 'hiero' key that overrides a Spring Security configuration property (e.g., hiero.mirror.web3.auth.enabled=false), disabling authentication for all users?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: convertPropertyName()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a property name like 'Hedera.Mirror.Something' (mixed case) to exploit the case-insensitive replace in convertPropertyName(), producing 'hiero.Mirror.Something' with inconsistent casing that fails to bind to the intended Spring configuration property, causing a silent misconfiguration?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: migrate()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a 'hedera'-prefixed property that, after migration, produces a 'hiero' key that conflicts with a Spring Boot auto-configuration property (e.g., hiero.datasource.url), causing the application to use an attacker-supplied database URL instead of the legitimate one?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/config/HieroPropertiesMigrator.java -> Scope: '] [Function: onApplicationEvent()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that HieroPropertiesMigrator runs at Ordered.LOWEST_PRECEDENCE to inject a 'hedera' property after other security-critical listeners have already read the environment, causing a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerability where the security configuration seen by early listeners differs from the final migrated configuration?
